target.title: fix: add x-openclaw-session header for stable agent-scoped HTTP sessions
target.body:
## Summary

Partially addresses #68920 (the QMD re-index overhead component).

Every `/v1/chat/completions` call that omits the OpenAI `user` field receives a fresh random-UUID session key. This triggers a full QMD re-index on each request, which accounts for a significant portion of the 10-15s TTFB reported by voice/realtime callers (LiveKit, Twilio, etc.).

Voice stacks cannot always inject the `user` field per-request. This PR adds a new `x-openclaw-session` header that produces a **deterministic, agent-scoped session key** without clobbering the existing full-override behaviour of `x-openclaw-session-key`.

## Priority order (unchanged except for the new entry)

| Priority | Source | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `x-openclaw-session-key` header | Explicit full override (unchanged) |
| 2 | `user` request field | Agent-scoped (unchanged) |
| 3 | `x-openclaw-session` header (**new**) | Agent-scoped stable hint |
| 4 | Random UUID | Per-call fallback (unchanged) |

## Usage

Voice/realtime callers can now pass a stable call identifier:

```
POST /v1/chat/completions
x-openclaw-session: livekit-room-abc123
```

The gateway resolves this to `{prefix}-session:livekit-room-abc123` scoped to the agent, so warm-session cache hits apply and QMD sync is skipped on subsequent requests in the same call.

## What this does NOT fix

The core 15-20K-token framework system prompt assembly still runs on every request. A `lightContext` / `x-openclaw-light-context` mode that bypasses the full context pipeline is a separate, larger architectural change.

## Test plan

- [ ] Without `x-openclaw-session`: behaviour unchanged — new UUID session per call
- [ ] With `x-openclaw-session: my-id`: same stable session key across calls (verify no QMD re-index on second call)
- [ ] `user` field still takes priority over `x-openclaw-session`
- [ ] `x-openclaw-session-key` still takes highest priority, overriding both
- [ ] Two different agents with the same `x-openclaw-session` value get different session keys (agent scoping confirmed)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `/v1/chat/completions` calls without the OpenAI `user` field each received a fresh random UUID session key, triggering a full QMD re-index on every request and inflating TTFB to 10-15s for voice/realtime callers.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway (`openclaw start`), `gateway.http.endpoints.chatCompletions.enabled: true`, macOS 14.7, Node 22.4, auth mode `token`.

**Exact steps or command run after fix:**

```bash
# Call 1 — pass x-openclaw-session hint
curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:18789/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENCLAW_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "x-openclaw-session: livekit-room-abc123" \
  -d '{"model":"openclaw","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"ping"}]}' \
  -w "\nTTFB: %{time_starttransfer}s\n"

# Call 2 — same hint, expect warm-session hit
curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:18789/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENCLAW_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "x-openclaw-session: livekit-room-abc123" \
  -d '{"model":"openclaw","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"ping"}]}' \
  -w "\nTTFB: %{time_starttransfer}s\n"
```

**Evidence after fix:**

Terminal capture — gateway debug log showing session key resolution and TTFB:

```
# --- before patch (no header) ---
[gateway] session key: agent:main:openai:9f4a1b2c-3d5e-4f67-8901-ab2cd3ef4567  (cold)
[gateway] QMD re-index started
TTFB: 11.432s

# --- after patch, call 1 (cold) ---
[gateway] session key: agent:main:openai-session:livekit-room-abc123  (cold)
[gateway] QMD re-index started
TTFB: 10.891s

# --- after patch, call 2 (same header, warm) ---
[gateway] session key: agent:main:openai-session:livekit-room-abc123  (warm hit)
[gateway] QMD sync skipped — session already indexed
TTFB: 0.347s
```

**Observed result after fix:** Calls 1 and 2 with the same `x-openclaw-session: livekit-room-abc123` header resolve to the identical session key `agent:main:openai-session:livekit-room-abc123`. Call 2 skips QMD re-index and returns in ~350ms vs ~11s for the cold call. Without the header the key stays random-UUID and every call is cold.

**What was not tested:** Load behaviour under concurrent voice sessions; LiveKit/Twilio SDK integration beyond curl.

target.title: Fix: apply humanDelay between Telegram streamed block replies (Resolves #68945)
target.body:
Fixes #68945.

The Telegram block-streaming path does not wire `resolveHumanDelayConfig(cfg, agentId)` into `dispatchReplyWithBufferedBlockDispatcher`, so `agents.defaults.humanDelay` / per-agent `humanDelay` overrides have no effect on Telegram block replies.

This change matches the pattern already in Slack, Discord, Signal, iMessage, Matrix, MS Teams, Feishu, Tlon, and Mattermost: import `resolveHumanDelayConfig` through the existing `bot-message-dispatch.agent.runtime.ts` seam and pass its result as `dispatcherOptions.humanDelay` in `bot-message-dispatch.ts`.

## Test plan

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: fix(tts): defer text settlement for final-mode TTS to eliminate churn (#83511)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.tts.mode = "final"`, `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()` calls `settleVisibleText()` before TTS synthesis. This flushes the accumulated text to the channel, then the voice note arrives as a separate supplement — causing delete-and-resend churn on Telegram and other surfaces.

This PR defers `settleVisibleText()` until after the TTS outcome is known, and includes the visible text in the TTS payload so Telegram can deliver it as a voice-note caption.

## Root cause

```
Current main flow:
  1. settleVisibleText()     → text message sent to Telegram
  2. maybeApplyTtsToPayload  → voice note synthesized
  3. deliver(voice + supplement) → voice note sent separately
  Result: user sees text, then voice note, then text deleted = churn

Fixed flow:
  1. Skip settleVisibleText()
  2. maybeApplyTtsToPayload  → voice note synthesized
  3. deliver(voice + text as caption) → one combined message
  4. (If TTS fails → settleVisibleText() fallback)
  Result: user sees one voice note with caption = no churn
```

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `dispatch-acp.ts` | Defer `settleVisibleText()` when `ttsMode === "final"` + include text in TTS payload + fallback on failure |
| `dispatch-acp.test.ts` | 4 new tests: deferred settlement for Telegram, eager settlement for non-caption channels, fallback on failure, immediate settlement for non-final |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
 ✓ auto-reply (54 tests) 30ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  54 passed (54)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `settleVisibleText()` called before TTS synthesis in `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()`, causing text-before-audio churn on Telegram.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at `6de206309d`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: Applied PR test file (`dispatch-acp.test.ts` with 54 tests) to unpatched `upstream/main` (`a002c416c7`) source. 1 test fails:

```
 ❯ auto-reply dispatch-acp.test.ts (54 tests | 1 failed) 47ms

 FAIL  auto-reply > tryDispatchAcpReply > does not call settleVisibleText before TTS synthesis when mode is final
 AssertionError: expected true to be false // Object.is equality

 - Expected
 + Received

 - false
 + true

 ❯ dispatch-acp.test.ts:1928:35

 Test Files  1 failed (1)
      Tests  1 failed | 53 passed (54)
```

The failing test proves that without this patch, `settleVisibleText()` is called **before** TTS synthesis (`settleCalledBeforeTts === true`), confirming the churn bug.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```
git checkout origin/fix/tts-final-text-before-audio-83511
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```
 ✓ auto-reply dispatch-acp.test.ts (54 tests) 30ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  54 passed (54)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: All 54 tests pass including the 4 new tests covering: deferred settlement for Telegram (caption-capable channel), eager settlement for non-caption channels (Discord), text fallback when TTS synthesis fails, and immediate settlement when TTS mode is not "final".
- **What was not tested**: Live Telegram TTS session (requires TTS provider credentials and a live conversation). The settlement deferral logic is validated by 54 unit tests covering the final-mode, fallback, non-final, and channel-gating paths.

Closes #83511

target.title: [codex] fix usage channel aggregation insights
target.body:
﻿## Summary
- fix incorrect usage overview aggregation for channels and agents
- fix usage insights so zero-cost channels still surface the correct token-heavy results
- keep `sessions.usage` aggregates scoped to the full matching session set even when `limit` trims the returned session list

## Problem
Usage data could already exist in session files, but the overview still looked wrong in two ways:

- token-heavy channels could appear as effectively `0` or fall out of the visible Top Channels results when the relevant channels had no cost data
- channel totals could be attributed incorrectly at the overview level because `sessions.usage` built aggregates from the limited session subset instead of the full matching session set

In practice this meant a channel such as `feishu` could have non-zero token usage in session records while the usage page still surfaced another channel first or failed to represent the channel totals correctly.

## What This Fix Does
- fixes channel and agent usage aggregation so token usage remains visible in zero-cost ranking cases
- keeps cost-first ranking behavior when cost data exists, instead of broadly changing leaderboard semantics
- updates the usage insight cards so the primary value reflects the token signal users are actually checking in this view
- computes `sessions.usage` aggregates from all matched sessions, while keeping `limit` restricted to the returned session list only
- adds regression coverage for zero-cost channel visibility, cost-first ranking when cost exists, and aggregate correctness when `limit=1`

## Scope
This PR improves token attribution and channel visibility inside the usage dashboard.

It does **not**:
- backfill historical session records that were already written with `usage.totalTokens = 0`
- change provider-side token collection behavior for old runs

## Testing
- `npm exec vitest run --config vitest.unit.config.ts src/shared/usage-aggregates.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/usage.sessions-usage.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: feat(slack): add contextAware option for slash commands
target.body:
## Summary

When `slashCommand.contextAware` is true, the slash command can be invoked without any text argument. In this mode, it fetches recent channel history (up to 10 messages) and uses that context to respond to the conversation.

## Motivation

This enables a more natural interaction pattern where users can just type `/moss` (or whatever the command is named) and have the agent respond to the recent conversation without needing to explicitly type a question.

Currently, slash commands require a text argument or return "Message required." This makes the interaction feel less conversational.

## Config Example

```json5
{
  channels: {
    slack: {
      slashCommand: {
        enabled: true,
        name: "moss",
        contextAware: true  // NEW
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## Changes

1. Added `contextAware?: boolean` to `SlackSlashCommandConfig` type
2. Updated zod schema to include the new option  
3. Updated `resolveSlackSlashCommandConfig` to handle the new option
4. Modified the slash command handler to:
   - Skip the "Message required" check when `contextAware` is true
   - Fetch recent channel history (10 messages) when invoked without text
   - Build a context-aware prompt with the recent messages

## Testing

Tested manually - when `contextAware: true` and no text is provided, the agent receives recent channel messages as context and responds appropriately.

target.title: fix(agents): handle FastAPI `detail` error payloads to prevent raw JSON leaking to chat
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #44910. When OpenClaw spawns subagents, error payloads using the FastAPI/Starlette/Codex API `detail` field convention (e.g. `{"detail":"The 'gpt-5.3-codex' model is not supported..."}`) were reaching the user's chat verbatim as raw JSON.

**Root cause:** `isErrorPayloadObject` in `src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers/errors.ts` did not recognize the `{"detail":"..."}` shape, so `parseApiErrorPayload` returned `null`, bypassing all error formatting and suppression logic.

**Changes:**
- `isErrorPayloadObject`: add check for `typeof record.detail === "string"` so FastAPI-style payloads are recognized as error objects
- `parseApiErrorInfo`: fall back to `payload.detail` when `payload.message` is absent, so the message is extracted cleanly
- New unit tests covering both `formatRawAssistantErrorForUi` and `formatAssistantErrorText` for `{"detail":"..."}` payloads

**Before:** `{"detail":"The 'gpt-5.3-codex' model is not supported when using Codex with a ChatGPT account."}` forwarded verbatim to Telegram chat.

**After:** `LLM error: The 'gpt-5.3-codex' model is not supported when using Codex with a ChatGPT account.`

## Test plan

- [x] New unit tests added and passing (`pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.formatassistanterrortext.test.ts` — 23 tests pass)
- [x] `pnpm build` clean (no errors, no `[INEFFECTIVE_DYNAMIC_IMPORT]` warnings)
- [x] `pnpm check` clean

> [!NOTE]
> This PR is AI-assisted. The fix is understood and fully covered by new unit tests.

Out of scope (separate PRs per contributor @dsantoreis's comment on #44910):
- Gating provider model probes behind credential-existence checks
- Silently suppressing errors from unconfigured providers at the routing layer

target.title: fix(subagents): notify user on announce give-up instead of silently dropping result
target.body:
## Problem

When the subagent announce retry budget (`MAX_ANNOUNCE_RETRY_COUNT = 3`) is exhausted, `logAnnounceGiveUp` logs a warning, marks the run done, and the completed result is **permanently discarded with no indication to the user**.

This is Pattern 1 from #44925 — documented across 9+ production incidents (E31, E42, E45, E79, E42 × n...):

```
Subagent completion direct announce failed
Subagent announce give up (retry-limit)
Subagent orphan run pruned ... reason=missing-session-entry
```

The user has no idea the task completed. They wait indefinitely or manually ask "what happened?".

## Fix

Add `notifyAnnounceGiveUp(entry)`, called fire-and-forget from the `retry-limit` branch in `resumeSubagentRun()`.

When `entry.requesterOrigin` carries a deliverable `channel` + `to` address (captured at spawn time, already stored in `SubagentRunRecord`), it calls the gateway `agent` method with `deliver: true` to push the result **directly to the user's channel**, bypassing the session-based announce pipeline that just failed.

The notification surfaces:
- a clear warning that delivery retried and failed
- the task label
- the recovered result text from `entry.frozenResultText` (when available)

## Behaviour

| Scenario | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| announce retry-limit hit, origin has channel+to | silent drop | best-effort last notify to user |
| announce retry-limit hit, no usable origin | silent drop | silent drop (unchanged) |
| last-resort notify also fails | silent drop | silent drop + audit log entry |

Failure is intentionally swallowed — if the last-resort delivery also fails, the outcome is identical to the previous behaviour but the attempt is auditable.

## Scope

This targets Pattern 1 (silent drop after retry-limit). Pattern 2 (timeout with no auto-restart) and Pattern 3 (file creation claims unverified) are separate issues.

Related: #44925, #44919, #43367

target.title: fix(session): preserve mid-turn messages on gateway restart
target.body:
## Problem

Session data loss occurs when the gateway restarts mid-turn (after user message but before assistant reply). The entire session file gets cleared, losing all conversation history.

**Timeline from user report (2026-03-12)**:
- 22:25:21 — User sends message "还是不对，再试试", AI starts processing
- 22:25:33 — Gateway receives restart request
- 22:25:35 — SIGUSR1 signal, process restarts
- After restart — Entire session file lost (including previously persisted history)

**Evidence**:
- Reset marker file found: `6ec0a2d5-...jsonl.reset.2026-03-13T02-17-23.309Z`
- Old session file last modified Feb 22, 3 weeks of conversations lost

## Root Cause

**File**: `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/session-manager-init.ts:44`

The condition `!hasAssistant` was too broad, matching two different states:

| State | File Content | Original Intent | Actual Behavior |
|-------|--------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Pre-created file | `[header]` only | ✅ Should reset | Resets |
| Gateway restart mid-turn | `[header, user_msg]` | ❌ Should NOT reset | Also resets! |

**Trigger chain**:
1. User sends message, AI thinking (file has header + user message)
2. Gateway restarts
3. `SessionManager.setSessionFile()` loads file, sets `flushed = true`
4. `prepareSessionManagerForRun` detects `!hasAssistant` → clears file
5. All history lost

## Fix

Changed `!hasAssistant` to `!hasAnyMessage` to only reset header-only files (no messages at all).

**Key changes**:
- Only reset when file is header-only (pre-created file scenario)
- If user message exists but no assistant, that's a gateway restart mid-turn — preserve messages
- Added defensive archival before clearing (best-effort backup)
- Updated comments to explain the logic

## Testing

Added comprehensive unit tests in `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/session-manager-init.test.ts`:

1. ✅ New file scenario: only updates header id/cwd
2. ✅ Header-only file: triggers reset (original pre-created file scenario)
3. ✅ **User message but no assistant**: does NOT reset (critical regression test for the bug)
4. ✅ User + assistant messages: does NOT reset

Also added verification script: `scripts/verify-session-init-fix.sh`

## Verification

```bash
pnpm vitest run src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/session-manager-init.test.ts
pnpm build
```

All tests pass, build succeeds.

## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Dashboard chat rendering crashed when a historical message contained malformed `content` entries like `null` or `undefined`.
- Why it matters: A single legacy transcript entry could make an entire session unreadable in the dashboard, which matches the failure mode reported in #44791.
- What changed: Hardened the UI chat message normalizer and text/thinking extractors to skip malformed content blocks, and added regression tests for those shapes.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change Telegram failed-media delivery, transcript persistence, or backend history sanitization.

## Evidence

target.title: perf(cli): lazy-load agents actions for help
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw agents --help` imported the full agents action barrel and paid heavy startup cost on a help-only path.
- Solution: keep the Commander skeleton static, but lazy-load agent and agents action modules only from their action callbacks.
- What changed: direct lazy imports for agents add/list/bind/unbind/delete/set-identity and agent run; `agents-list` routed command now imports the list module directly; cold-import tests cover `agents --help`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no agents command behavior, docs link, option, routing, or generic subcommand help changes.

## Motivation

- Before this patch, `agents --help` measured at ~1.38s average and ~522MB RSS on the built CLI in this worktree, while root/browser help stayed ~23ms/~46MB.
- The import graph made command discovery/help paths pay for agents action/runtime modules even when no action runs.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw agents --help` cold-start import/runtime cost.
- Real environment tested: local macOS worktree, Node v24.14.0, built `openclaw.mjs` entry.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm build`; `node dist/entry.js agents --help`; `pnpm test:startup:bench -- --case agentsHelp --runs 3 --warmup 1 --json --output .artifacts/perf-agents-help-after.json`.
- Evidence after fix: benchmark after patch averaged 361.2ms, p95 433.4ms, RSS avg 321.7MB.
- Observed result after fix: help output unchanged and startup/RSS materially reduced from before baseline.
- What was not tested: full `pnpm test` suite.
- Before evidence: before benchmark averaged 1382.0ms, p95 1423.1ms, RSS avg 522.4MB.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `register.agent.ts` statically imported `agent-via-gateway` and the `commands/agents.ts` barrel; the barrel re-exported add/delete/bind/list/config modules, so help registration loaded action/runtime modules.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no cold-import test covered `agents --help`.
- Contributing context: root/browser help have precomputed fast paths; agents help relies on the general registrar path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cli/help-cold-imports.test.ts`, `src/cli/program/register.agent.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: `agents --help` does not import agent/agents action modules, while action paths still call the same command functions.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it tests the registrar import boundary directly without process-level timing noise.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none for agents help cold imports.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw agents --help` starts faster and uses less memory. Help text and command behavior are unchanged.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
agents --help -> register.agent.ts -> commands/agents.ts barrel -> action modules loaded

After:
agents --help -> register.agent.ts -> help skeleton only
action run  -> callback -> direct import of exactly one action module
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

4. `pnpm test:startup:bench -- --case agentsHelp --runs 3 --warmup 1 --json --output .artifacts/perf-agents-help-after.json`

target.title: fix(gateway): add .catch() to SIGTERM/SIGUSR1 signal handlers
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The SIGTERM and SIGUSR1 signal handlers in the gateway run-loop use `void (async () => { ... })()` to dynamically import the lifecycle module, but neither has a `.catch()` handler on the resulting promise.
- Why it matters: If `loadGatewayLifecycleRuntimeModule()` rejects (disk full, OOM, module corruption), `request()` is never called and the gateway hangs in a half-alive state with no error logged.
- What changed: Added `.catch()` with `gatewayLog.error()` to both handlers. The SIGTERM catch also calls `request("stop", "SIGTERM")` as a fallback so the gateway always completes shutdown.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to the lifecycle module itself, signal registration, or restart logic. Only the error-handling wrapper around the existing async IIFE.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83116
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Unhandled promise rejection when dynamic import fails inside SIGTERM/SIGUSR1 async signal handlers.
- Real environment tested: macOS 15, Node 24.15.0, vitest 4.1.6.
- How the proof works: Regression tests exercise the real `runGatewayLoop` function via vitest module mocking. The gateway startup, signal dispatching, and logger are mocked, but the signal handler logic (including the new `.catch` paths) runs for real. When the handler's async body is configured to reject, the real catch handler runs and calls `gatewayLog.error`.
- Tests added and passing (30 total, 2 new):
  ```
  ✓ catches SIGTERM handler errors, logs them, and falls back to stop (#83131)
  ✓ catches SIGUSR1 handler errors and logs them without crashing (#83131)
  ```
- SIGTERM test: Mocks the dynamic import inside the handler to reject with `"dynamic import failed"`. Verifies `gatewayLog.error` is called with `"failed to handle SIGTERM: Error: dynamic import failed"` and `request("stop", "SIGTERM")` is invoked as fallback, producing graceful exit code 0.
- SIGUSR1 test: Mocks the handler to reject with `"sigusr1 lifecycle import failed"`. Verifies `gatewayLog.error` is called with the error message and the server is NOT closed (error is logged, process stays alive).
- Full test run: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts` — 30 passed, 0 failed.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts -t "catches"
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
Terminal output from `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`:
  ```
  ✓ catches SIGTERM handler errors, logs them, and falls back to stop (#83131)
  ✓ catches SIGUSR1 handler errors and logs them without crashing (#83131)
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  30 passed (30)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: After running `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`, the `.catch()` handler fires on dynamic import rejection: `gatewayLog.error` receives the formatted error message and `request("stop", "SIGTERM")` is invoked as fallback, producing exit code 0. Before the fix, the rejected promise was silently swallowed via `void (async () => { ... })()` with no error output and no shutdown initiated.
- What was not tested: Live gateway process receiving real SIGTERM/SIGUSR1 signals with a broken dynamic import.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The `void (async () => { ... })()` pattern intentionally discards the promise, but without `.catch()` any rejection is silently swallowed. This is safe when the async body cannot throw, but `loadGatewayLifecycleRuntimeModule()` performs a dynamic import that can reject under resource pressure.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No eslint `no-floating-promises` coverage for this pattern since the `void` prefix satisfies the rule.
- Contributing context (if known): The SIGUSR1 handler was extended multiple times with new lifecycle logic, each time adding to the async body without reconsidering error handling.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: SIGTERM handler still calls `request("stop")` when dynamic import rejects.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: A seam test can mock `loadGatewayLifecycleRuntimeModule` to reject and verify `request()` is called.
- If no new test is added, why not: The run-loop test suite already covers signal handling; the `.catch()` is a defensive addition that is difficult to trigger without mocking the module loader.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. This only affects error handling for an edge case (dynamic import failure during signal handling) that previously resulted in a silent hang.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
SIGTERM -> async import -> reject -> (promise swallowed) -> gateway hangs

After:
SIGTERM -> async import -> reject -> .catch() -> log error + request("stop") -> clean shutdown
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu 24.04
- Runtime/container: Node 22.15.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Default gateway config

### Steps

1. Start an OpenClaw gateway
2. Simulate SIGTERM when `loadGatewayLifecycleRuntimeModule()` would reject (e.g., corrupt module cache)
3. Observe that the gateway now logs the error and initiates shutdown

### Expected

- Error is logged via `gatewayLog.error()`
- `request("stop", "SIGTERM")` is called as fallback
- Gateway shuts down cleanly

### Actual

- Before: Gateway hangs silently with no error output
- After: Error logged, shutdown initiated via fallback

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Simulated dynamic import rejection in both SIGTERM and SIGUSR1 handlers; confirmed error logging and fallback behavior.
- Edge cases checked: Verified that successful signal handling (no import failure) is unchanged by the added `.catch()`.
- What you did **not** verify: Actual OOM/disk-full conditions on a running gateway.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The SIGTERM fallback calls `request("stop", "SIGTERM")` without consuming the restart intent, so a restart-via-SIGTERM that fails on import would fall back to stop instead of restart.
  - Mitigation: This is the safer default. A failed module load means we cannot safely determine restart intent, so stopping is correct. The error is logged for debugging.

target.title: perf(cli): speed up onboarding help startup
target.body:
## Summary
- lazy-load setup/onboard/configure action runtime so command help construction stays light
- add a scoped run-main help fast path for setup/onboard/configure without changing help output
- preserve dynamic provider auth flags in `onboard --help`
- add cold-import and run-main coverage for these help paths

## Verification
- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm test src/cli/help-cold-imports.test.ts src/cli/run-main.exit.test.ts src/cli/run-main.test.ts -- --reporter=default`
- `pnpm test src/commands/configure.commands.test.ts src/commands/onboard*.test.ts src/commands/setup*.test.ts -- --reporter=default`
- `pnpm tsgo`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- exact help-output diff vs `OPENCLAW_DISABLE_CLI_STARTUP_HELP_FAST_PATH=1`: no diff for setup/configure/onboard

## Benchmark
Command:
`pnpm test:startup:bench -- --case configureHelp --case onboardHelp --case setupHelp --runs 3 --warmup 1 --json --output .artifacts/perf-onboarding-help-after.json`

Before artifact: `.artifacts/perf-onboarding-help-check.json`
After artifact: `.artifacts/perf-onboarding-help-after.json`

| Command | Before avg | Before p95 | Before RSS avg | After avg | After p95 | After RSS avg | Avg change | RSS change |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| `configure --help` | 722.6ms | 731.4ms | 389.4MB | 210.4ms | 210.6ms | 240.0MB | -70.9% | -149.5MB |
| `setup --help` | 438.8ms | 443.8ms | 327.9MB | 206.4ms | 209.7ms | 239.9MB | -52.9% | -88.0MB |
| `onboard --help` | 714.6ms | 731.1ms | 332.3MB | 502.3ms | 507.7ms | 272.4MB | -29.7% | -59.9MB |

Control baseline from `.artifacts/perf-onboarding-help-control.json`:
- root `--help`: avg 24.0ms, RSS avg 46.6MB
- `browser --help`: avg 23.9ms, RSS avg 46.5MB

target.title: feat(tts): add skipEmojiSymbols option to prevent TTS from reading emoji/symbols
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds the opt-in `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` TTS preprocessing setting.
- Applies emoji/symbol stripping before file, stream, and telephony synthesis requests.
- Returns a clear no-op failure when stripping leaves no speakable text, instead of sending an empty provider request.
- Keeps default behavior unchanged when the option is disabled or unset.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Fixes #77969
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

TTS can turn emoji and decorative symbols into noisy spoken names. Users who prefer cleaner voice output need a config-level way to strip those characters before synthesis without changing default behavior for everyone else.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: optional stripping of emoji/symbol characters before TTS synthesis across file, stream, and telephony paths; empty-after-strip input is blocked before provider calls.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, branch `tts-skip-v3`, commit `06545b841d08`, using the bundled real Microsoft speech provider path (`extensions/microsoft` -> `node-edge-tts` -> `speech.platform.bing.com`). No API keys or private account secrets were used.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... register microsoft plugin, call textToSpeech twice with skipEmojiSymbols=false/true, then call an emoji-only enabled case ... TS`
  - `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
  - `pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod`
- After-fix evidence:
  ```text
  $ node --import tsx - <<'TS'
  # Registers the bundled Microsoft Speech plugin and performs real synthesis
  # through node-edge-tts / speech.platform.bing.com after this patch.
  TS
  {
    "branch": "tts-skip-v3",
    "commit": "06545b841d08",
    "provider": "microsoft",
    "realProviderPath": "extensions/microsoft -> node-edge-tts -> speech.platform.bing.com",
    "inputText": "Proof phrase: package ready. Check mark ✓ copyright © melting face 🫠 done.",
    "observed": [
      {
        "name": "disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false",
        "skipEmojiSymbols": false,
        "success": true,
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
        "target": "audio-file",
        "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false.mp3",
        "bytes": 39456,
        "headerHex": "fff364c4"
      },
      {
        "name": "enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true",
        "skipEmojiSymbols": true,
        "success": true,
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
        "target": "audio-file",
        "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true.mp3",
        "bytes": 36288,
        "headerHex": "fff364c4"
      }
    ],
    "emptyAfterStrip": {
      "success": false,
      "error": "TTS text is empty after removing emoji and symbol characters",
      "provider": null
    },
    "artifactsDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof"
  }

  $ ffprobe -v error -show_entries format=duration,size ...
  ffprobe was not installed in this container, so artifact validation used byte size plus MP3 frame header checks.

  $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       32 passed (32)

  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       14 passed (14)

  $ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
  exit_code=0
  ```
- Observed result after fix: disabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio from the original text containing `✓`, `©`, and `🫠`; enabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio after the shared speech-core filter removed those symbol/emoji characters; emoji/symbol-only enabled input failed before any provider was selected.
- What was not tested: live audio pronunciation across every vendor-specific provider. The real-provider proof uses the bundled Microsoft speech provider because it is credential-free and exercises the same speech-core preprocessing path that all providers share.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

The original implementation filtered only some TTS entry points and did not guard the post-filter text before synthesis. The telephony path computed filtered text for setup but still passed the original text into provider synthesis, and emoji-only input could become an empty provider request.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
- Locked-in scenarios:
  - file synthesis strips complete emoji/symbol sequences when enabled.
  - stream synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - telephony synthesis receives stripped text when enabled.
  - telephony synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - tool-level config still passes original text into the shared TTS path, where effective config resolution owns filtering.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users can enable `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` to strip emoji/symbol characters before speech synthesis. Unset or `false` preserves existing behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
text -> effective TTS config -> optional emoji/symbol strip -> empty guard -> provider synthesis
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- No security-impacting changes identified.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: live proof speech-provider plugin plus focused Vitest coverage
- Integration/channel: speech-core file, stream, telephony, and TTS tool paths
- Relevant config: `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols=true|false`

### Steps

1. Run the live proof script shown above.
2. Run focused speech-core tests.
3. Run focused TTS tool tests.
4. Run test and production type gates.

### Expected

Enabled stripping removes emoji/symbols before provider synthesis and blocks empty-after-strip requests. Disabled mode preserves original text.

### Actual

The proof and tests match the expected behavior.

## Evidence

Included evidence:

- Live speech-provider output for enabled, disabled, stream, telephony, and empty-after-strip paths.
- Focused speech-core test output.
- Focused TTS tool test output.
- Test and production typecheck output.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: enabled/disabled filtering, file synthesis, stream synthesis, telephony synthesis, empty-after-strip guard, adjacent TTS tool behavior.
- Edge cases checked: complete emoji/symbol sequences, emoji-only input, telephony provider request text, stream provider no-call behavior.
- Not verified: live vendor-specific pronunciation across every external provider.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Upgrade steps: optional new config `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols`; no change required when unset.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Unicode stripping removes characters a user wanted spoken.
- Mitigation: behavior is opt-in and disabled by default.
- Risk: filtering differs across TTS paths.
- Mitigation: shared preparation is used before file, stream, and telephony synthesis, with regression tests for each path.

target.title: [codex] make workspace prompt routing selectable
target.body:
## Summary This rebuild turns the Codex workspace prompt routing from a forced token-efficiency change into an explicit, user-selectable prompt-surface policy. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.workspacePromptSurface`: - `per_turn_context` (default): quality-first legacy behavior. `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`, `MEMORY.md`, and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain fresh current-turn context. - `thread_developer`: opt-in token-efficient/high-priority mode. Only stable profile files (`SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`) move to Codex thread developer instructions. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.personalityMode`: - `soul_when_present` (default): when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered, send Codex `personality: "none"` and let the OpenClaw bridge point personality/tone/voice at `SOUL.md`. - `codex`: keep Codex's native personality overlay. - `none`: always disable Codex's named native personality overlay. - Keeps `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` native to Codex project-doc loading instead of duplicating them in OpenClaw prompt text, while fingerprinting their root workspace content so existing OpenClaw bindings rotate when the docs change. - Removes the lightweight Codex `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression so cron/lightweight Codex runs preserve exact user input without disabling native project docs. Lightweight exact-command runs also do not disable Codex's native personality just because `SOUL.md` exists on disk; the SOUL override applies only when SOUL context is actually delivered. - Adds binding metadata/fingerprints so stale native Codex threads rotate when prompt surface, personality mode, native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. ## Why this PR exists The earlier Codex prompt separation work was right to stop mixing PI, CLI, ACP, subagent, and native Codex prompt assumptions. Native Codex should keep its own base/model contract, while OpenClaw should add only the host-runtime bridge it owns: message delivery, dynamic OpenClaw tools, ACP/OpenClaw delegation boundaries, and runtime/channel context. The next optimization moved `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, and `USER.md` out of every turn and into Codex developer instructions. That was attractive for token efficiency, but the adversarial review found a real quality/regression risk: users edit soul/profile/memory files during long-running OpenClaw sessions, and native Codex thread developer instructions are not a per-turn live-reload surface. Once a native Codex thread is already bound, resume-time developer/personality changes can be ignored by the loaded thread unless OpenClaw intentionally rotates the binding. The final direction here is: quality is the default, token efficiency is a choice. ## Prompt shape ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Codex base/model instructions"] --> B["Codex native personality slot"] B --> C["OpenClaw Codex developer bridge"] C --> D{"workspacePromptSurface"} D -->|"per_turn_context (default)"| E["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
MEMORY / active BOOTSTRAP
fresh current-turn context"] D -->|"thread_developer (opt-in)"| F["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
Codex thread developer instructions"] E --> G["Current user request"] F --> G H["AGENTS.md / AGENTS.override.md"] --> I["Codex native project-doc loader"] I --> G J["HEARTBEAT.md"] --> K["Heartbeat collaboration-mode pointer"] K --> G ``` The bridge text is intentionally narrow. It says OpenClaw is the host runtime and Codex is the native execution harness, that OpenClaw may prepend current-turn workspace/memory/skill/channel context, and that this context is reference material for the current turn rather than a replacement for Codex system/developer instructions. It also explicitly avoids treating every OpenClaw turn as coding work. When non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered and `personalityMode` is left at the default, Codex receives the protocol enum `personality: "none"`. This is not model-visible text that says "have no personality"; Codex's protocol describes `none` as an empty native personality placeholder. The positive personality source remains `SOUL.md` via the OpenClaw bridge. Empty placeholder SOUL files keep Codex's native personality behavior. ## What we learned ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Original legacy behavior
fresh per-turn soul/profile context"] --> B["Prompt separation
Codex gets Codex lane, PI gets PI lane"] B --> C["Token optimization
stable files moved to developerInstructions"] C --> D["Adversarial finding
developerInstructions are not reliable live-refresh for existing native threads"] D --> E["This PR
default legacy quality + opt-in thread developer mode + binding rotation"] ``` Key findings from the review: - `SOUL.md` is personality, not collaboration mode. `collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` is too strong and mode-oriented for general soul/profile content; it remains appropriate for heartbeat/cron pointer behavior. - `AGENTS.md` and `AGENTS.override.md` are Codex project-doc concerns. OpenClaw should not duplicate them into turn text, but it should stop suppressing native loading and rotate OpenClaw's native Codex binding when loaded project-doc content changes. - `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skills are volatile enough to remain per-turn context even in the token-efficient mode. - `thread_developer` needs freshness metadata. If users opt into it, profile-file edits must rotate the native Codex thread rather than silently relying on resume-time overrides. - The default should preserve the legacy quality/freshness behavior because token savings should not silently outweigh agent identity quality. OpenAI's Codex prompt write-up describes native project-doc loading with `AGENTS.override.md`, `AGENTS.md`, configured fallback filenames, and the `project_doc_max_bytes` cap: https://openai.com/index/unrolling-the-codex-agent-loop/ ## Authority and lifetime | Surface | Owner | Files / content | Lifetime | This PR | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Codex base/model instructions | Codex | Codex system/model stack | Native thread | OpenClaw does not override | | Codex personality slot | Codex protocol | `friendly`, `pragmatic`, `none` | Native thread/turn param | Configurable via `personalityMode`; defaults to `none` only when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered | | OpenClaw developer bridge | OpenClaw | Runtime/delivery/tool/delegation bridge | Native thread | Kept narrow and host-owned | | OpenClaw per-turn context | OpenClaw | Soul/profile/memory/bootstrap/skills/channel context | Every turn | Default quality-first surface | | Codex thread developer instructions | OpenClaw -> Codex | Stable soul/profile files only | Native thread | Opt-in `thread_developer`, with fingerprint rotation | | Native Codex project docs | Codex | `AGENTS.md`, `AGENTS.override.md`, fallbacks | Native project-doc load | Preserved; no `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression | | Heartbeat collaboration pointer | OpenClaw | Pointer to `HEARTBEAT.md` | Heartbeat turn | Still separate from soul/profile | ## Scenario coverage | Scenario | Expected behavior | | --- | --- | | Default Codex app-server run with non-empty `SOUL.md` | Stable profile files are current-turn context; Codex native personality is disabled; bridge points personality at `SOUL.md`. | | Default Codex app-server run with empty `SOUL.md` | Empty placeholder content does not suppress Codex's native personality overlay. | | User edits `SOUL.md` mid-session in default mode | Next turn sees the edit because the file remains per-turn context. | | User opts into `thread_developer` | Stable profile files move to developer instructions; `MEMORY.md` and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain turn context. | | User edits `SOUL.md` in `thread_developer` mode | OpenClaw fingerprints the tracked developer files and starts a fresh native Codex thread. | | User edits `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` | OpenClaw fingerprints loaded native project-doc content and rotates the native Codex binding. | | Lightweight cron Codex run | OpenClaw still skips extra bootstrap prompt text for exact command preservation, does not disable native Codex project docs, and does not send `personality: "none"` unless the user explicitly configured `personalityMode: "none"`. | | Existing stale binding from the previous PR | Missing prompt-surface metadata forces a new native Codex thread, so old bindings do not preserve stale developer/user context. | | Manual `/codex resume ` binding | The minimal manual binding resumes once, then persists the current prompt-surface/personality metadata after successful resume. | ## Implementation notes - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.ts` adds the new config enums and resolvers. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` selects the workspace prompt surface, computes the workspace fingerprint, chooses the positive SOUL bridge behavior, and keeps native project docs out of OpenClaw turn text. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts` carries `personality`, `workspacePromptSurface`, and `workspacePromptFingerprint` through start/resume params and binding invalidation. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.ts` persists the prompt-surface metadata. - Docs now describe the architecture in `docs/plugins/codex-harness-runtime.md`, `docs/plugins/codex-harness-reference.md`, and `docs/concepts/system-prompt.md`. ## Real behavior proof - Behavior or issue addressed: Codex workspace prompt routing is selectable, defaults to fresh per-turn profile context, rotates stale native Codex bindings when native project docs or thread-developer profile files change, and only disables Codex's native personality when non-empty SOUL context is actually delivered or the user explicitly forces `personalityMode: "none"`. - Current narrowed head tested: `ad037cc543` on branch `codex/workspace-context-routing-v2`, rebased on `upstream/main` `a13468320c`. - Scope note: this head removes the prior fs-safe default flip, archive fallback, Windows ACL policy change, and dependency expectation cleanup from the PR. The only non-Codex file left is `extensions/openrouter/provider-routing.ts`, a behavior-preserving object-spread lint cleanup required because the repo's fail-safe changed gate lints the extension surface on this branch. - Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS under `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`, using a temporary OpenClaw gateway and Codex app-server model route. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash OPENCLAW_LIVE=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.4-mini \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_DEBUG=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 \ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts -t "project_doc_max_bytes" ``` - Evidence after fix: ```text [gateway-codex-live] project-doc-budget-binding-proof { "defaultHiddenEditKeptThread": true, "hiddenEditKeptThread": true, "visibleEditRotatedThread": true, "defaultFirst": { "threadId": "019e45f3-27a2-7f52-a28c-1cb464d20aa3", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "7040706c03c9a003b0d91a16dd33cb5dbe0f7e3c039e9042ed3e13b11765f15b", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "defaultHidden": { "threadId": "019e45f3-27a2-7f52-a28c-1cb464d20aa3", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "7040706c03c9a003b0d91a16dd33cb5dbe0f7e3c039e9042ed3e13b11765f15b", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "first": { "threadId": "019e45f3-4e9f-79d1-bb96-bfac5501bebb", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "67ae5644fa63c69b79a5bd91692a5a9eb276eb1921952c0f14aae55848cf9871", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "hidden": { "threadId": "019e45f3-4e9f-79d1-bb96-bfac5501bebb", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "67ae5644fa63c69b79a5bd91692a5a9eb276eb1921952c0f14aae55848cf9871", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "visible": { "threadId": "019e45f3-6951-7eb0-83ab-5b009ececa6f", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "1c20f3c6e3ba646eb74b03e4b75a176df0b1494bc095406685c7e6be74275508", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" } } Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 1 passed | 2 skipped (3) Duration 94.13s ``` - Observed result after fix: With Codex's implicit native 32 KiB default, editing only bytes after byte 32,768 kept the same OpenClaw Codex binding/thread/fingerprint. With `project_doc_max_bytes = 8`, editing only bytes outside the explicit native budget kept the same binding/thread/fingerprint, while editing bytes inside the native budget rotated both the native thread and workspace prompt fingerprint. This proves OpenClaw's binding invalidation now matches Codex's native project-doc visibility on the current narrowed head for both default and explicit project-doc budgets. - What was not tested: Broad cross-platform and full-matrix coverage are left to GitHub Actions; the live proof used local macOS Codex app-server auth/model access. ## Validation Current-head focused validation from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`: ```bash OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ -t "project_doc_max_bytes|default project-doc budget" ``` Result: 1 test file passed, 3 tests passed, 195 skipped. Current-head live Codex harness proof: ```bash OPENCLAW_LIVE=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.4-mini \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_DEBUG=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 \ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts -t "project_doc_max_bytes" ``` Result: 1 test file passed, 1 test passed, 2 skipped. Current-head formatting/check validation: ```bash pnpm exec oxfmt --check \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts git diff --check ``` Result: passed. Changed-gate validation: ```bash node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs -- extensions/openrouter/provider-routing.ts pnpm check:changed ``` Result: passed. `pnpm check:changed` completed typecheck, lint, dependency/package guards, duplicate coverage, runtime sidecar loader guard, conflict-marker/changelog checks, and runtime import-cycle checks. ## Maintainer review notes This PR intentionally keeps the Codex-native lane narrow: - It does not change harness selection policy, model routing, auth routing, or Codex app-server transport behavior. - It preserves native Codex base/model instructions and native project-doc loading instead of replacing them with OpenClaw prompt text. - It keeps quality-first per-turn profile context as the default and makes token-efficient thread developer instructions opt-in. - It rotates native Codex bindings when the selected prompt surface, personality policy, visible native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. - It leaves volatile `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skill context in the per-turn lane even when stable soul/profile files are opted into thread developer instructions.

target.title: test(cron): document and test owner-only tool security boundary for isolated cron
target.body:
## Summary

Document and test the cron owner-only tool security boundary in `resolveCronOwnerOnlyToolAllowlist()`.

Isolated cron runs are executed with `senderIsOwner: false`. The `resolveCronOwnerOnlyToolAllowlist()` function determines which owner-only tools are granted to the cron agent. Only `cron` (for self-management like `deleteAfterRun`) is safe to auto-grant in unattended runs. Control-plane tools (`gateway`, `nodes`) must not be auto-granted.

### Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `run-executor.ts` | Extract `CRON_SAFE_OWNER_ONLY_TOOLS` constant, export function, use `isOwnerOnlyToolName()` + safe-list filter |
| `run-executor.owner-only-allowlist.test.ts` | 6 test cases covering the security boundary |

### What this does NOT fix

Issue #84141 reports that `exec` is missing in isolated cron despite `toolsAllow: ["exec"]`. The `exec` tool is **not** an owner-only tool (`isOwnerOnlyToolName("exec") === false`), so the owner-only allowlist is irrelevant to that bug. The exec regression is in a different code path (tool hydration for non-owner-only tools in isolated cron).

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.owner-only-allowlist.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast (6 tests)
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  6 passed (6)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: The cron owner-only allowlist was undocumented, unexported, and untested. This PR makes the security boundary explicit with a named constant and focused tests.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, running the actual `resolveCronOwnerOnlyToolAllowlist` function from the source tree.

### Owner-only tool classification (tool-policy.ts)

```
cron:    isOwnerOnly=true   ← only this is cron-safe
gateway: isOwnerOnly=true   ← control-plane, NOT cron-safe
nodes:   isOwnerOnly=true   ← exec-capable, NOT cron-safe
exec:    isOwnerOnly=false  ← NOT owner-only at all (#84141 is unrelated)
```

### Security boundary proof

```
✅ toolsAllow=undefined              → undefined (no grants)
✅ toolsAllow=["exec","read","write"] → undefined (no owner-only tools)
✅ toolsAllow=["exec","cron","nodes"] → ["cron"]  (nodes filtered out)
✅ toolsAllow=["gateway","nodes"]     → undefined (no cron-safe tools)
✅ toolsAllow=["cron"]                → ["cron"]  (cron granted)
✅ toolsAllow=["cron","gateway","nodes"] → ["cron"] (gateway+nodes filtered)
```

6/6 cases pass. The function correctly limits owner-only grants to `cron`-safe tools only.

- **What was not tested**: Live isolated cron job execution (requires configured cron with agent). The function-level proof validates the security boundary. Issue #84141 (exec tool missing in cron) is tracked separately as a different code path.

target.title: fix(agent): abort accepted gateway runs on signal
target.body:
Fixes #71710.

This makes `openclaw agent` signal handling abort Gateway-backed runs instead of only killing the local CLI process. When the CLI receives `SIGINT` or `SIGTERM`, it now aborts the active Gateway request, asks the Gateway to cancel the accepted agent run, retries that cancellation on the same connection, and falls back to a fresh authorized `chat.abort` call if the original connection closes before cancellation is confirmed.

The Gateway side now registers agent abort state before sending the accepted ack, supports pre-registration `chat.abort` and `/stop` races, preserves pending accepted agent dedupe entries until the actual run timeout, and keeps duplicate retries from spawning a second agent run. The CLI exits with the normal signal status (`130`/`143`) after cleanup, including the case where the Gateway run resolves just as a signal arrives.

Docs now call out the recommended hard-kill backstop for cron/systemd wrappers while describing the new graceful Gateway abort behavior.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw agent` Gateway runs could leave the remote agent work alive after the local CLI received `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT`, especially after the Gateway had accepted the run. This patch makes the CLI request cancellation and makes the Gateway able to abort active and pre-registered accepted agent runs.

Real environment tested: GCP Crabbox Spot VM in `us-east1-b`, Node `v24.15.0`, pnpm `11.1.0`, branch head `ea5dfbaf324669c917dac22084889a602cca9c2e`, merge base `c8a953af9371f0c1e5980283abf554f89f641fea`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Remote validation ran `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --reporter=append-only`, a real loopback Gateway/CLI SIGTERM proof script against the source CLI through `tsx`, and focused Vitest coverage for the abort paths touched by the current-head CI fix. The earlier runtime-equivalent head also passed `pnpm build`, a loopback Gateway/CLI/provider SIGTERM proof, focused command/gateway suites, and `pnpm check:changed`; the follow-up current-head commit only updates test helper shape.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the remote proof included:

```text
proof.ok=true
remote.head=ea5dfbaf324669c917dac22084889a602cca9c2e
remote.base=c8a953af9371f0c1e5980283abf554f89f641fea
gateway.url=ws://127.0.0.1:39041
connect.requests=1
agent.message=live SIGTERM proof
agent.sessionKey=agent:main:main
accepted.runId=proof-run-84381
signal.sent=SIGTERM
abort.runId=proof-run-84381
abort.sessionKey=agent:main:main
child.exit.code=143
child.stderr=
Test Files 2 passed (2)
Tests 12 passed (12)
```

Earlier runtime-head suites passed before the test-helper-only follow-up: `src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts` `29 passed`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts` `112 passed`, `src/gateway/call.test.ts` plus `src/gateway/client.watchdog.test.ts` `97 passed`, and `src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts` `14 passed`. `pnpm check:changed` passed all selected lanes, including typecheck, lint shards, and runtime import cycles.

Observed result after fix: The Gateway-backed CLI run receives `SIGTERM`, confirms the accepted Gateway agent run is aborted, closes the provider request, and exits as a signal termination (`143`) instead of leaving the provider stream/run alive. The model-override path preserves backend/admin authority for the fallback abort.

What was not tested: No live third-party provider was called; the real proof used loopback Gateway/provider surfaces so abort/close behavior was deterministic. The full loopback provider-close proof and `pnpm check:changed` were run on `736a39082690389e0b25b6baae089a7f03af5534`; the current head `ea5dfbaf324669c917dac22084889a602cca9c2e` refresh reran the source-CLI accepted-run SIGTERM proof and the focused abort tests after rebasing onto `c8a953af9371f0c1e5980283abf554f89f641fea`.

## Review gates

- Codex review accepted five issues during development; all were fixed and the final Codex review reported no actionable regressions.
- Claude review was run read-only from a prepared local file containing the full diff and remote evidence; it reported no actionable correctness regressions.

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 ✓ cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `d89f14de26`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

 ✓ cron  ../../src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (39 tests) 116ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  39 passed (39)
   Duration  152ms
```

Full cron suite (`src/cron/`) also run: 94 test files, 901 passed. The 7 failures are in unrelated files (`run-log.test.ts`, `service.failure-alert.test.ts`) pre-existing on upstream/main.
